parse.title: List of cities and municipalities in the Philippines
parse.wikitext:
{{Short description|none}} {{Use mdy dates|date=October 2022}} {{Use Philippine English|date=October 2022}} {{Philippine city and municipality tables sidebar}} This is a complete '''list of cities and municipalities in the Philippines'''. The [[Philippines]] is administratively divided into 82 [[Provinces of the Philippines|provinces]] ({{langx|fil|lalawigan}}). These, together with the [[Metro Manila|National Capital Region]], are further subdivided into [[Cities of the Philippines|cities]] ({{langx|fil|lungsod|link=no}}) and [[Municipalities of the Philippines|municipalities]] ({{langx|fil|bayan|link=no}}). Cities are classified under the Local Government Code of 1991 (Republic Act No. 7160) into three categories: highly urbanized cities, independent component cities, and component cities. Cities are governed by their own [[municipal charter]]s in addition to the Local Government Code of 1991, which specifies their administrative structure and powers. They are given a bigger share of the [[Internal Revenue Allotment]] (IRA) compared to regular municipalities. As of June 30, 2024, there are 149 cities (33 highly urbanized, 5 independent component, 111 component) and 1,493 municipalities encompassing the country. There are a total of 1,642 cities and municipalities in the Philippines.{{Cite web |date=June 30, 2024 |title=Regional Summary Number of Provinces, Cities, Municipalities and Barangays by Region |url=https://www.dilg.gov.ph/PDF_File/factsfigures/dilg-facts-figures-202489_c0319070a1.pdf |access-date=September 19, 2024 |website=Department of Interior and Local Government (DILG Philippines)}} {{Compact TOC|name={{nobold|by}} name {{nobold|(''only useful when the list is sorted alphabetically'')}}|left=yes|side=yes|x=X|y=Y}} {| class="toccolours" style="width:auto;" |- ! style="text-align:left;font-weight:normal;" | by '''province''' (''only useful when the list is sorted alphabetically by province''; default) |- | {{flatlist}}

scope="row" | [[Tungawan]] | 48,013 | 473.28 | 101.45 | 25 | Mun | [[Zamboanga Sibugay]] |} ==Chartered cities== This is a list of '''chartered cities in the Philippines'''. [[Cities of the Philippines|Philippine cities]] are classified into three groups: highly urbanized cities (HUC), independent component cities (ICC), and component cities (CC). ''Highly urbanized cities'' are local government units autonomous from provinces that have a minimum population of 200,000 and an annual income of at least {{₱|link=yes}} 50 million (in 1991 constant prices). ''Independent component cities'' are cities outside of provincial jurisdiction (although some are allowed to participate in the election of provincial officials) that have not yet attained the 'highly urbanized' status, while ''component cities'' are those under a province's jurisdiction. In addition, each city is classified into six income brackets according to income in a four-year period. For instance, 1st class cities have an income of {{₱}} 400 million or more, while 6th class cities earn less than {{₱}} 80 million in a four-year period. Each city is governed by both the Local Government Code of 1991 and the city's own [[municipal charter]], under the laws of the Philippines. There are 149 [[cities of the Philippines]] as of July 8, 2023. Thirty-four of these are ''highly urbanized cities'', four are ''independent component cities'', with the rest being ''component cities'' of their respective provinces. [[City charter]] documents include ''[[List of Philippine laws|Commonwealth Act]]'' (CA) and ''Republic Act'' (RA). Many pre-1987 cities had set dates of inauguration (where the president or a high-ranking government official participates in ceremonies marking the attainment of cityhood) or had set dates of organization (where the newly converted city officially starts to fully function as expected). The province indicated for such cities, as grouped by the [[Philippine Statistics Authority]], is in ''italics''.}} {{efn|name=mkt|Land area and population figures for Makati exclude Post Proper Northside and Post Proper Southside, which are still administered by Makati, but ruled in court to be part of Taguig in 2003.}} {{efn|name=psg|Population figure for Pasig excludes residents in areas disputed between this city and the municipality of [[Cainta]], Rizal.}} {{efn|name=tgg|Land area and population figures for Taguig include Post Proper Northside and Post Proper Southside, which are still administered by Makati, but ruled in court to be part of Taguig in 2023.}} {{efn|name=ang|Population figure for Angeles includes residents of disputed areas between its boundary with [[Mabalacat]].}} {{efn|name=mab|Population figure for Mabalacat excludes residents of disputed areas between its boundary with [[Angeles City]].}} {{efn|name=area|Land area figures are taken from the [[Philippine Statistics Authority]].}} {{efn|name=charter|Some of these city charters have been replaced, revised, or amended since. The link to the text of the original city charter is given if available online.}} {{efn|name=law|Most city charters were approved after being signed by the president. However, some were approved only after they lapsed into law, i.e. becoming law without the president's signature, which is possible if 30 days after being sent to the president's office for approval the act has remained unsigned.}} {{efn|name=eff|Before 1987, most municipalities gained cityhood immediately after the enactment or approval of their city charters. Only few pre-1987 city charters had a set date of effectivity (such as Tagbilaran's and Dipolog's) or required a plebiscite for the city to acquire corporate existence (such as Caloocan's or Laoag's). However, since 1987 city charters require ratification through a plebiscite, with a majority of votes cast by city residents affirming the charter. If not enough information is available, a charter's date of approval is assumed to be its date of effectivity, and the date is in ''italics''.}} }} ==See also== *[[List of cities and municipalities in the Philippines]] *[[List of metropolitan areas in the Philippines]] ==References== {{Reflist|30em|refs= {{cite PH act|chamber=RA|number=11938|url= https://www.officialgazette.gov.ph/downloads/2023/02feb/20230223-RA-11938-FRM.pdf |title= An Act Converting the Municipality of Carmona in the Province of Cavite into a component city to be known as the City of Carmona|date=February 24, 2023 |access-date=July 9, 2023 }} {{cite web | title = Republic Act No. 11544;
